questions.question: Which formula correctly expresses 'every dog is a mammal'?
questions.explanation: Universal statements use implication, not conjunction. `‚àÄx. Dog(x) ‚àß Mammal(x)` would mean 'everything is both a dog and a mammal' ‚Äî false. The implication form is vacuously true for non-dogs and asserts mammal-ness for dogs.

questions.question: What does `‚àÄx ‚àÉy. Loves(x, y)` say, contrasted with `‚àÉy ‚àÄx. Loves(x, y)`?
options: The first: everyone has someone they love (y may depend on x). The second: there is one specific person whom everyone loves.
Both require y to be unique
questions.explanation: Order of quantifiers matters. `‚àÄx ‚àÉy` lets y depend on x (different beloved per person). `‚àÉy ‚àÄx` demands a single y that works for every x. The second is much stronger. This distinction is central to cryptographic claims: 'for every adversary there exists a key the adversary can't break' is WEAKER than 'there exists a key no adversary can break.'

questions.question: What is the De Morgan equivalent of `¬¨‚àÄx. Even(x)`?
questions.explanation: `¬¨‚àÄx. P(x) ‚â° ‚àÉx. ¬¨P(x)`. 'Not everything is even' ‚áî 'something is not even'. Useful when pushing negation inward ‚Äî most proof systems prefer it that way.

questions.question: What does Skolemization replace?
options: Each existential `‚àÉy` with a function `f(...)` of the universally-quantified variables in scope, capturing y's dependence on them
questions.explanation: Skolemization eliminates existentials by introducing fresh function symbols. `‚àÄx ‚àÉy. P(x, y)` becomes `‚àÄx. P(x, f(x))` ‚Äî the f-function captures 'given x, the witness for y.' SMT solvers do this before CNF conversion. The Skolem function need not be defined; its existence is what's asserted.

questions.question: In `‚àÄx. (P(x, y) ‚Üí ‚àÉz. Q(x, z))`, which variables are free?
questions.explanation: x is bound by `‚àÄx`, z is bound by `‚àÉz`. y appears unquantified ‚Äî it's free. A closed formula has no free variables; this one isn't closed until y is bound (by an enclosing quantifier or by being a fixed constant in an interpretation).

questions.question: Why is `‚àÄx. P(x) ‚Üí Q(x)` logically equivalent to `¬¨‚àÉx. (P(x) ‚àß ¬¨Q(x))`?
options: Push negation inward via De Morgan + implication = ¬¨P ‚à® Q: ‚àÄx.(P(x)‚ÜíQ(x)) ‚â° ‚àÄx. ¬¨(P(x) ‚àß ¬¨Q(x)) ‚â° ¬¨‚àÉx. (P(x) ‚àß ¬¨Q(x))
questions.explanation: Step 1: P ‚Üí Q ‚â° ¬¨(P ‚àß ¬¨Q). Step 2: ‚àÄx. ¬¨R(x) ‚â° ¬¨‚àÉx. R(x). Compose: ‚àÄx. (P(x) ‚Üí Q(x)) ‚â° ‚àÄx. ¬¨(P(x) ‚àß ¬¨Q(x)) ‚â° ¬¨‚àÉx. (P(x) ‚àß ¬¨Q(x)). 'Every P is Q' is the same as 'no P is a non-Q.' Useful when searching for a counterexample to a universal claim.
